As Meta vies for a relatively small pool of machine‑learning PhDs, especially as other top labs have already drained much of the AI talent in the U.S., China can draw from a far bigger base of engineers and researchers trained at its top universities. When unleashed on ambitious projects like generative AI, this talent pool can achieve world‑class results quickly and with relatively fewer resources.

Second, government support in China will likely only accelerate the country’s AI development. The Chinese state has long identified AI as a national priority and has marshaled vast resources to back it. The success of DeepSeek has further piqued the Chinese government’s interest and drawn billions more dollars in state funding toward AI initiatives and the national push for tech self‑sufficiency. Unlike in the U.S., where open‑source AI is viewed warily by authorities, Beijing actively encourages an open ecosystem to Meta’s detriment.

By promoting open source, Chinese leaders sidestep U.S. sanctions on chips and software by tapping the global developer community to improve their models. This strategy effectively turns the worldwide AI community into collaborators on Chinese‑led models. Even major U.S. tech companies like Microsoft, Perplexity, and Meta itself have built upon DeepSeek’s open‑source models to remain competitive. The U.S. export bans expected to hamper China’s AI efforts have instead motivated Chinese AI labs to focus on efficiency breakthroughs through software and algorithm optimizations. In short, Chinese firms have found ways to do more with less, in stark contrast to Meta, which must rely primarily on its own R&D spending and limited talent pool.

Meta’s Financial Strength vs. Waning AI Leadership

Ironically, Meta’s core business continues to thrive even as its AI leadership in open source is challenged. The company closed out 2024 with impressive financial results. It reported full‑year revenue of $164.5 billion, a 22 percent year‑over‑year increase. Meta’s efficiency drive and focus on profitability also paid off with $62.4 billion in net income, up 59 percent year over year. Meta translated those earnings into $52.1 billion in cash flow, giving it more than enough resources to reinvest in technology. The company’s capital expenditures totaled $39.2 billion last year, among the highest of any tech firm.

However, this large spend may rapidly turn into a negative if Chinese AI labs like DeepSeek continue to outperform Meta’s LLaMA models. The new reality of incredible performance and hyper‑efficient Chinese open‑source models raises doubts about the efficiency and payoff of Meta’s AI spending. Meta finds itself in an arms race where its competitors are not other Western giants, but rivals that can achieve greater open‑source breakthroughs with an order of magnitude less capital and inferior chip technology. The cost advantage and speed of iteration in the Chinese open‑source community threaten to wipe out the benefits Meta expected from open sourcing its own models.

Potential Path Forward

While Meta has invested heavily in AI, the company was never fully on the LLM hype train. Its chief AI scientist, Yann LeCun, is known for skepticism around large language models. Meta has spent much of its resources on alternative architectures like V‑JPEA and LCM. If LeCun is correct that LLMs will eventually hit a wall, Meta’s current predicament is far less worrisome. In that scenario, Meta could be ahead of the competition, given its extensive research into non‑generative approaches.

Still, LLMs appear far from hitting any theoretical wall. The current reinforcement‑learning trajectories of these models seem to follow new exponential growth paths. For instance, OpenAI’s o4‑mini and o3 releases have again blown past expectations. Many argue we are seeing glimpses of artificial general intelligence with these models. Even if LLMs eventually hit a plateau, their capabilities are so vast that losing out on the LLM market could still have a large negative impact on Meta, especially in the near term.

Conclusion

While Meta’s fundamental business is stronger than ever, its path to AI leadership is murky at best. The open‑source revolution Meta hoped to lead is now driven by DeepSeek and Chinese tech giants. This does not spell doom for Meta, as the company still owns some of the world’s most popular social media platforms and generates profits on a scale few can match. Moreover, its war chest and commitment to AI—especially alternative architectures—could yield long‑term results. With that said, Meta is clearly no longer leading the race in open‑source language models, both in quality and efficiency. Given this dynamic, it would be more prudent to downgrade Meta to a hold at its current $1.27 trillion valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
